_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to solve linear inequalities and represent their solutions on a number line.

_source.w: We are learning to solve linear inequalities and represent their solutions on a number line.
y: Inequalities model situations where there is a range of acceptable values rather than a single answer — common in real-world constraints.
Understanding when the inequality sign reverses (multiplying or dividing by a negative) requires careful reasoning.
Inequalities are used in optimisation, engineering constraints, budgeting and decision-making.
eg: Solving: 3x + 7 < 22 → 3x < 15 → x < 5, and representing on a number line with an open circle at 5.
Solving: –2x + 5 ≥ 11 → –2x ≥ 6 → x ≤ –3 (sign reverses when dividing by negative).
Solving a contextual problem: 'How many items can be purchased if total cost must be under $200?'
Graphing inequality solutions on a number line and interpreting them in context.
